key_albums: Call the Doctor (1996, Kill Rock Stars)
Dig Me Out (1997, Kill Rock Stars)
All Hands on the Bad One (2002)

theory_nodes.concept: The two-guitar no-bass architecture: the most significant structural innovation in Pacific Northwest indie rock of the 1990s. No conventional role division (rhythm/lead) -- both guitars in perpetual motion, interplay as content, no background only foreground. Low-end distributed between Tucker's lower-register guitar and Weiss's aggressive kick drum. Sonic density in upper-mid frequency (200-2,000 Hz) rather than sub-bass. The architecture is complete, not compensating for an absence.
theory_nodes.description: No-bass two-guitar architecture -- interplay as content, distributed low-end, complete not compensating.

theory_nodes.concept: Carrie Brownstein guitar biography: parts that do not function exclusively as rhythm or lead -- both simultaneously. Low end provides rhythmic grounding; upper register provides melodic commentary; continuous rapid transition between. Guitar doing work normally distributed across lead player, rhythm player, and bassist. Vocal harmonies structural not decorative.
theory_nodes.description: Carrie Brownstein -- simultaneous rhythm/lead, transition as technique, structural harmonies.

theory_nodes.concept: Corin Tucker vocal biography: vibrato not trained but organic, idiosyncratic, immediately identifiable. Not conventionally beautiful in radio-format sense; emotionally precise. Vibrato is the sound of pitch's instability under pressure of song's emotional content -- not ornamental. Guitar approach rhythmically direct, complementary to Brownstein's architectural complexity.
theory_nodes.description: Corin Tucker -- organic vibrato, emotional precision, rhythmic directness complementing Brownstein.

theory_nodes.concept: Janet Weiss drums biography: joined 1996 completing Dig Me Out lineup; third voice in three-voice ensemble; not timekeeping but melodic/textural participant; kick drum carries low-frequency absent bass; heavier and more present than typical rock drumming because it carries low end the no-bass decision removed. Restraint and precision as defining qualities alongside force.
theory_nodes.description: Janet Weiss -- third voice, kick drum carries low-end, heavier presence by necessity, restraint and force.

theory_nodes.concept: Dig Me Out (1997) peak calibration: John Goodmanson production transparent; 34 minutes 13 tracks; Start Together establishes two-guitar architecture in opening 10 seconds; One More Hour vocal and guitar interplay maximum; peak-period specificity -- all elements at fullest development; durability as calibration indicator (most consistently cited Sleater-Kinney album in retrospective assessments).
theory_nodes.description: Dig Me Out -- Goodmanson production, peak-period specificity, durability as calibration.

theory_nodes.concept: Kill Rock Stars Olympia sequence: Slim Moon's label documented the Olympia tradition's fullest development. Degrees 357-359 all Kill Rock Stars Olympia. The label's roster (Bikini Kill, Sleater-Kinney, Unwound, Elliott Smith) represents the Pacific Northwest underground's most significant non-Sub Pop bodies of work in the 1990s.
theory_nodes.description: Kill Rock Stars Olympia sequence -- three final degrees, alternative to Sub Pop, Slim Moon.

cross_references.relationship: Bikini Kill origin-point of riot grrrl and feminist punk tradition; Sleater-Kinney develops it musically into the most architecturally sophisticated output. Both Olympia, both Kill Rock Stars, both E=10.
Same label, same city, different approach: experimental/ambient vs architectural post-punk. Both E=10 as the fullest development of their respective traditions. The Kill Rock Stars Olympia trilogy.

Both use guitar architecture as primary structural innovation. Botch: non-coincident two-guitar voices (Knudson + Latona). Sleater-Kinney: no-bass two-guitar interplay. Different implementations of the two-guitar innovation. Different contexts, same principle.

sps_pair.pairing_note: E=10 + E=10 = E=20. Simultaneous distinct voices in distinct registers creating a whole exceeding parts. Three Sleater-Kinney instruments in distinct frequency registers: Brownstein guitar (architectural, wide-register), Tucker guitar+voice (rhythmic, vibrato, distinctive), Weiss drums (third voice). All-species dawn chorus: thrushes (1-3 kHz), warblers (2-5 kHz), wrens (4-8 kHz), frogs (2-4 kHz), seals (0.1-1.5 kHz). Both are E=10 through architectural convergence.
